---
title: "AD698 - Applied Generative AI"
subtitle: "Neural Network Math and SGD"
logo: "../theme/figures/met_logotype_black.png"
date: 03/12/2024
date-modified: today
date-format: long
author:
  - name: Nakul R. Padalkar
    affiliations:
      - id: BU
        name: Boston University
        city: Boston
        state: MA
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [../theme/presentation.scss]
    html-math-method: katex
    slide-number: c/t
from: markdown+emoji
self-contained-math: true
fig-align: center
monofont: Roboto
title-slide-attributes:
  data-background-image: "../theme/blank_red.png"
  data-background-size: 103% auto
  data-background-opacity: "0.95"
execute:
  echo: false
  eval: false
  warning: false
  message: false
categories: ['M0:', 'Slides', 'Presentation']
description: "Presentation slides for Module 0 on Mathematical Foundations for Neural Networks and SGD."
---

## Why This Lecture Matters

The goal of Module 0 math is not abstract theorem proving.
The goal is to make neural-network equations, optimization logic, and PyTorch training code readable and intuitive.

---

## Roadmap

- Scalars, vectors, matrices, and tensors
- Derivatives, gradients, and chain rule
- Activation functions
- Softmax and cross-entropy
- Gradient descent vs. SGD
- Why these ideas show up directly in PyTorch

---

## Mathematical Preliminaries

- Scalar = single value
- Vector = ordered list
- Matrix = 2D array
- Tensor = higher-dimensional generalization

Why this matters:

- shapes encode meaning
- shape mismatches are often math mistakes, not just code bugs

---

## Linear Algebra Intuition

For a linear model:

$$
\hat{y} = Xw + b
$$

This simple equation already introduces the core objects used throughout deep learning:

- input matrix
- parameter vector
- bias term
- prediction output

---

## Derivative and Gradient

- Derivative gives slope in 1D
- Gradient generalizes this to multiple parameters

$$
\nabla f(\theta)=\left[\frac{\partial f}{\partial \theta_1},\dots,\frac{\partial f}{\partial \theta_p}\right]^\top
$$

The negative gradient gives the local direction of steepest decrease.

---

## Chain Rule and Backpropagation

If a loss depends on nested intermediate computations, gradients propagate backward using the chain rule:

$$
\frac{\partial L}{\partial w} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial z}\frac{\partial z}{\partial w}
$$

Backpropagation is just the efficient repeated application of this rule through the network.

---

## Why Activation Functions Matter

Without nonlinearity, multiple stacked linear layers collapse into one linear map.

That means:

- no extra expressive power
- limited ability to model complex patterns

Activations make deep learning deep in a meaningful way.

---

## Common Activations

```python
sigmoid = 1 / (1 + np.exp(-x))
tanh = np.tanh(x)
relu = np.maximum(0, x)
```

Interpretation:

- **Sigmoid**: bounded between 0 and 1, useful for binary outputs
- **Tanh**: zero-centered, but still saturates
- **ReLU**: sparse and computationally simple, strong hidden-layer default

---

## Activation Plot

![Common Activation Functions](./M0_lecture03_figures/fig_activations.png){width="85%"}

This visual is reinforced in Tutorial 2 and Lab 2, where students compare activation behavior directly.

---

## Softmax and Classification

For multiclass prediction, logits are transformed into probabilities:

$$
\text{softmax}(z_i)=\frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}}
$$

Properties:

- outputs are positive
- outputs sum to 1
- larger logit gaps produce more confident predictions

---

## Cross-Entropy Loss

$$
\mathcal{L} = -\sum_{k=1}^{K} y_k \log \hat{p}_k
$$

Why we use it:

- natural loss for multiclass classification
- heavily penalizes confident wrong predictions
- connects directly to maximum likelihood

---

## Optimization Objective

Training means minimizing empirical risk:

$$
\min_\theta J(\theta)=\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\ell(f_\theta(x_i), y_i)
$$

This objective shows up again in Module 0 labs and assignments when students train regression and classification models.

---

## Gradient Descent vs. SGD

| Method | Uses | Tradeoff |
|---|---|---|
| Full-batch GD | all samples each step | stable but expensive |
| SGD / mini-batch SGD | subset of samples | faster, noisier, practical |

In practice, mini-batch SGD is the default because it scales better.

---

## Learning Rate Intuition

From Tutorial 2 and Lab 2:

- too small: learning is slow
- too large: updates overshoot or diverge
- moderate: loss decreases steadily

This is one of the first hyperparameter tradeoffs students should learn to reason about.

---

## Why This Lecture Connects to Code

Every major math object has a PyTorch counterpart:

- vector or matrix -> `torch.tensor`
- gradient -> `.grad`
- chain rule -> `autograd`
- SGD update -> optimizer step
- softmax and cross-entropy -> built-in loss functions

That translation from math to code is the real goal of this lecture.

---

## Course Alignment

- **Tutorial 2** turns these ideas into small experiments
- **Lab 2** asks students to visualize activations and compare SGD behavior
- **Assignment 0** expects them to interpret optimization and model results, not just run code

---

## Key Takeaways

- Deep learning depends on linear algebra, calculus, and optimization
- Gradients and chain rule are the foundation of backpropagation
- Activations create expressive nonlinear models
- Softmax and cross-entropy are central for classification
- SGD is the practical optimization workhorse students will keep using
